questions.question_text: Which of the following variables is **numerical**?

options.text: Time taken to run $100$ m
Postcode, such as $2450$
options.why: A postcode is written with digits but only labels an area — it is never measured or counted, so it is categorical.

options.text: Jersey number worn by a player
options.why: A jersey number is a label written with digits; you never measure or count it, so it is categorical.

options.text: Phone number
options.why: A phone number identifies a line; its digits are a label, not a measured or counted amount, so it is categorical.

questions.solution_text:
A postcode, jersey number and phone number are labels written with digits — they are never measured or counted, so they are categorical.
Time is **measured**, giving a number.
**Time taken to run $100$ m** is numerical.

questions.question_text: Which of the following variables is **categorical**?

options.text: Favourite music genre
Number of songs on a playlist
options.why: This is a count, so it is numerical, not categorical.

options.text: Length of a song in seconds
options.why: This is measured, so it is numerical, not categorical.

options.text: Age in years
options.why: Age is counted or measured as a number, so it is numerical; grouping ages into bands does not change the variable.

questions.solution_text:
Number of songs is counted, and song length and age are measured — all numerical.
A favourite genre **names a group**, so it is categorical.
**Favourite music genre** is categorical.

questions.question_text: Which of the following survey questions would generate **numerical** data?

options.text: How long does it take you to travel to school?
What is your favourite colour?
options.why: A colour names a group, so this generates categorical data, not numerical.

options.text: What type of car does your family own?
options.why: A car type is a label for a group, so this generates categorical data, not numerical.

options.text: What breed of dog do you own?
options.why: A breed names a group, so this generates categorical data, not numerical.

questions.solution_text:
Colour, car type and dog breed each name a group, so they give categorical data.
A travel time is **measured** as a number.
The question about travel time generates **numerical** data.

questions.question_text: A teacher records each student's favourite radio station, such as $101.7$ and $104.9$. How should this variable be classified?

options.text: Categorical, because a station number only labels the station and is never measured or counted.
Numerical, because the stations are written as numbers.
options.why: Assumes anything written with digits is numerical; the number only labels the station.

options.text: Numerical, because you could work out an average station number.
options.why: You can compute an average of the digits, but it is meaningless — the values are labels, not measurements.

options.text: Numerical, because each station has a decimal value.
options.why: Treats the decimal frequency as a measured quantity; it is a fixed label for the station, not a measurement of anything.

questions.solution_text:
A radio station number is a **label** that identifies the station, not something measured or counted.
Adding or averaging station numbers would be meaningless.
**Categorical.**

questions.question_text: Which of the following survey questions would generate **categorical** data?

options.text: Which is your favourite fast food?
How many times a week do you eat fast food?
options.why: This is a count, so the data is numerical, not categorical.

options.text: How much do you spend on fast food?
options.why: An amount of money is measured, so the data is numerical, not categorical.

options.text: How many items did you buy last time?
options.why: This is a count, so the data is numerical, not categorical.

questions.solution_text:
Counting visits or items and measuring money spent all give numerical data.
Naming a favourite fast food **names a group**.
The favourite fast food question generates **categorical** data.
